'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]
{{Gakkōrei}} {{Education-stub}}

On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].

'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}} == See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}} == See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.

revision.text:
'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}} == See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]
{{Gakkōrei}} {{Education-stub}}

'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. == First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.

== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

{{draft categories| [[Category:Abolished Japanese Imperial Ordinances]] [[Category:Former educational institutions in Japan]] [[Category:Secondary education in Japan]] [[Category:Women’s education in Japan]] [[Category:1886 laws]] [[Category:1886 in Japan]] [[Category:1886 in April]] [[Category:1899 laws]] [[Category:1899 in Japan]] [[Category:1899 in February]] [[Category:1943 repeals]] [[Category:Middle School Ordinance]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Middle School Ordinance]] [[ja:中学校令]] [[ja:v]] [[sn:iddle School Ordinance]] [[en:Middle School Ordinance]]

'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

revision.text:
{{日本の法令 | 題名=中学校令 | 効力=廃止 | 種類=[[教育法]] | 内容=[[旧制中学校]]の規定 | 関連=[[高等女学校令]]、[[実業学校令]]、[[中等学校令]] | リンク=[{{NDLDC|2947969/1}} 官報 1899年2月7日] | ウィキソース=中学校令 }} '''中学校令'''（ちゅうがっこうれい、明治32年2月7日勅令第28号）は、[[近代]][[日本]]の[[中等教育|中等教育機関]]のうち、[[旧制中学校|中学校]]（いわゆる旧制中学校）を規定していた[[勅令]]である。
==歴史== [[森有礼]][[文部大臣]]の下、[[1886年]]（[[明治]]19年）[[4月10日]]に、それまでの[[教育令]]を廃し、いわゆる「[[学校令]]」の一つとして公布された（'''第一次中学校令'''、明治19年4月10日勅令第15号）。その後[[1899年]]（明治32年）[[2月7日]]に全部改正され（'''第二次中学校令'''、明治32年2月7日勅令第28号）、[[1943年]]（昭和18年）[[1月21日]]に公布された[[中等学校令]]（昭和18年勅令第36号）によって廃止されるまで、50年以上効力を有した。
==第一次中学校令== ;公布日 *1886年（明治19年）4月10日（明治19年勅令第15号） ;概要 *[[旧制中学校|中学校]]の性質を「'''実業に就きたいと思う者または高等の学校に入学したいと思う者に必要な教育を行う場所'''」とする。 *編制は中学校を'''[[高等中学校]]'''と'''尋常中学校'''の2等に分ける。 **'''高等中学校''' ***文部大臣が管理し、'''全国に5校'''設置する。経費は国庫とその区内における府県の地方税とによって支出することとする。
*'''尋常中学校''' **各府県において設置することができるが、地方費の支出または補助によるものは'''各府県1ヶ所'''に限り、区町村費で設置することはできない。（'''一府県一校設置の原則'''） **中学校令に基づき、「尋常中学校ノ学科及其程度」が制定され以下のことが規定された。 ***[[修業年限]]を'''5年'''とする。5年を1級～5級にわけ、毎級の授業年限を1年とする。 ***入学資格を'''12歳以上'''の中学校予備の小学校またはそのほかの学校の卒業者とする。 ***学科を倫理以下普通学科目15科目とし、そのうち第二外国語と農業を選択科目とする。また土地の状況・事情によっては文部大臣の認可をうけることによって商業・工業の科を設置することができる。
;一部改正 *[[1891年]]（明治24年）[[12月14日]] **尋常中学校の設置条件を'''緩和'''。 ***土地の状況により、各府県に数校の尋常中学校を設置することができる。また1校も設置しなくてもよい。 ***郡市町村においては、区域内の小学校教育の施設上妨げとならない場合に尋常中学校を設置することができる。 ***尋常中学校に農業・工業・商業等の専修科を設置することができる。 **'''[[高等女学校]]を尋常中学校の一種'''と明文化。
==第二次中学校令== ;公布日 *1899年（明治32年）2月7日（明治32年勅令第28号） ;変更点・概要 *尋常中学校の名称を'''中学校'''に改称<ref>[[1893年]]（明治27年）[[6月25日]]に[[高等学校令]]が公布され、従来上段階を占めていた高等中学校が[[旧制高等学校|高等学校]]として分離し、中学校は尋常中学校だけとなっていたため。</ref>。 *目的を「男子に必要な高等普通教育を行うこと」と規定。 *修業年限を5年とし、1年以内の補習科を設置することができる。 *入学資格は12歳以上で[[高等小学校]]第2学年課程を修了した者とする。 *設置に関し、各府県に対して「'''1校以上の中学校を設置しなければならない'''」として中学校設置を義務づけた。文部大臣が必要と認めた場合府県に中学校の増設を命じることができるようにして、中学校設置に対する積極的姿勢を明らかにした。 *郡市町村や町村学校組合にも、より容易に中学校設置が認められる。 *文部大臣の許可を受ければ、1校につき1分校の設置ができる。 *同日[[高等女学校令]]が公布されたため、中学校令より高等女学校の記述（第14条）が除かれる。
;結果 *中学校増設の機会を多くしたことにより、次第に上級学校進学の気運が高められ、中学校の急速な発達が促された。
;一部改正 *[[1907年]]（明治40年）[[7月18日]] -「中学校令中改正ノ件」（明治40年勅令280号） **'''第10条（入学資格）''' - [[義務教育|義務]]年限の延長<ref>それまで尋常小学校4年間が義務年限であったが、[[小学校令]]の改正に伴い、義務年限が2年延長され、6年となった。尋常小学校4年・高等小学校4年であった修業年限が、尋常小学校6年・高等小学校2年に改められた。</ref>に伴い、12歳以上で'''尋常小学校卒業者'''と改める。 *[[1919年]]（[[大正]]8年）[[2月7日]] -「中学校令中改正ノ件」（大正8年勅令11号） **中学校設置に関して市町村学校組合を加えて設置の主体を拡張。 **中学校に[[予科]]を設置することができ、その入学資格を小学校卒業者と同等以上の学カがあると認められる者とする。 **1919年（大正8年）[[3月29日]]に「中学校令施行規則」を制定し、予科の修業年限を2年とし、その入学資格を10歳以上・[[尋常小学校]]4学年修了者とした。中学校入学資格に関して、尋常小学校5年の課程を修了し、学業優秀かつ身体の発育十分で中学校課程を修了できると学校長が証明した者は受験することができるとした。 *[[1941年]]（[[昭和]]16年）3月1日 -「中学校令中改正ノ件」（昭和16年勅令第151号） **'''第10条（入学資格）'''- [[国民学校令]]（昭和16年勅令第148号）に基づき、[[尋常小学校]]を[[国民学校]]初等科に改める。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== {{Wikisource|中学校令}} *[[文部省]] *[[旧制中学校]] *[[旧制高等学校]] *[[高等女学校令]] *[[実業学校令]] *[[高等中学校令]] *[[中等学校令]] *[[学校教育法]]
==外部リンク== *[https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm 学制百年史] - [[文部科学省]]
{{学校令}} {{Education-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ちゆうかつこうれい}} [[Category:廃止された日本の勅令]] [[Category:日本の旧制教育機関の法令]] [[Category:日本の旧制中学校|令*]] [[Category:日本の高等女学校|令ちゆうかつこう]] [[Category:日本の旧制高等学校|令*ちゆうかつこう]] [[Category:日本の女性教育の歴史]] [[Category:1886年の法]] [[Category:1886年の日本]] [[Category:1886年4月]] [[Category:1899年の法]] [[Category:1899年の日本]] [[Category:1899年2月]] [[Category:1943年に廃止された法]]

==第二次中学校令== ;公布日 *1899年（明治32年）2月7日（明治32年勅令第28号） ;変更点・概要 *尋常中学校の名称を'''中学校'''に改称<ref>[[1893年]]（明治27年）[[6月25日]]に[[高等学校令]]が公布され、従来上段階を占めていた高等中学校が[[旧制高等学校|高等学校]]として分離し、中学校は尋常中学校だけとなっていたため。</ref>。 *目的を「男子に必要な高等普通教育を行うこと」と規定。 *修業年限を5年とし、1年以内の補習科を設置することができる。 *入学資格は12歳以上で[[高等小学校]]第2学年課程を修了した者とする。 *設置に関し、各府県に対して「'''1校以上の中学校を設置しなければならない'''」として中学校設置を義務づけた。文部大臣が必要と認めた場合府県に中学校の増設を命じることができるようにして、中学校設置に対する積極的姿勢を明らかにした。 *郡市町村や町村学校組合にも、より容易に中学校設置が認められる。 *文部大臣の許可を受ければ、1校につき1分校の設置ができる。 *同日[[高等女学校令]]が公布されたため、中学校令より高等女学校の記述（第14条）が除かれる。
;結果 *中学校増設の機会を多くしたことにより、次第に上級学校進学の気運が高められ、中学校の急速な発達が促された。
;一部改正 *[[1907年]]（明治40年）[[7月18日]] -「中学校令中改正ノ件」（明治40年勅令280号） **'''第10条（入学資格）''' - [[義務教育|義務]]年限の延長<ref>それまで尋常小学校4年間が義務年限であったが、[[小学校令]]の改正に伴い、義務年限が2年延長され、6年となった。尋常小学校4年・高等小学校4年であった修業年限が、尋常小学校6年・高等小学校2年に改められた。</ref>に伴い、12歳以上で'''尋常小学校卒業者'''と改める。 *[[1919年]]（[[大正]]8年）[[2月7日]] -「中学校令中改正ノ件」（大正8年勅令11号） **中学校設置に関して市町村学校組合を加えて設置の主体を拡張。 **中学校に[[予科]]を設置することができ、その入学資格を小学校卒業者と同等以上の学カがあると認められる者とする。 **1919年（大正8年）[[3月29日]]に「中学校令施行規則」を制定し、予科の修業年限を2年とし、その入学資格を10歳以上・[[尋常小学校]]4学年修了者とした。中学校入学資格に関して、尋常小学校5年の課程を修了し、学業優秀かつ身体の発育十分で中学校課程を修了できると学校長が証明した者は受験することができるとした。 *[[1941年]]（[[昭和]]16年）3月1日 -「中学校令中改正ノ件」（昭和16年勅令第151号） **'''第10条（入学資格）'''- [[国民学校令]]（昭和16年勅令第148号）に基づき、[[尋常小学校]]を[[国民学校]]初等科に改める。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== {{Wikisource|中学校令}} *[[文部省]] *[[旧制中学校]] *[[旧制高等学校]] *[[高等女学校令]] *[[実業学校令]] *[[高等中学校令]] *[[中等学校令]] *[[学校教育法]]
==外部リンク== *[https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm 学制百年史] - [[文部科学省]]

revision.text:
'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Abolished Japanese Imperial Ordinances
revision.text:
'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Women’s education in Japan
revision.text:
'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].

== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[Ministry of Education (Japan)]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
