'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[:en:Yoka|Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11363851]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[:en:Yoka|Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[:en:Yoka|Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
== External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology]]

Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[:en:Yoka|Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11363851]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[:en:Yoka|Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11363851]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]] on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In [[:en:1893|1893]] (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended [[:en:Compulsory education|Compulsory education]] term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ([[:en:Yoka|Yoka]]) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture]] * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In {{ill|1893|en|1893|WD=Q7840|lt=1893}} (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended {{ill|Compulsory education|en|Compulsory education|WD=Q511009|lt=Compulsory education}} term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ({{ill|Yoka|en|Yoka|WD=Q20661847|lt=Yoka}}) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|WD=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}} * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education {{ill|Mori Arinori|en|Mori Arinori|WD=Q1194472|lt=Mori Arinori}} on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. ; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended {{ill|Compulsory education|en|Compulsory education|WD=Q511009|lt=Compulsory education}} term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ({{ill|Yoka|en|Yoka|WD=Q20661847|lt=Yoka}}) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].

; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended {{ill|Compulsory education|en|Compulsory education|WD=Q511009|lt=Compulsory education}} term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ({{ill|Yoka|en|Yoka|WD=Q20661847|lt=Yoka}}) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|WD=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}} * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education {{ill|Mori Arinori|en|Mori Arinori|WD=Q1194472|lt=Mori Arinori}} on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In {{ill|1893|en|1893|WD=Q7840|lt=1893}} (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended {{ill|Compulsory education|en|Compulsory education|WD=Q511009|lt=Compulsory education}} term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ({{ill|Yoka|en|Yoka|WD=Q20661847|lt=Yoka}}) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|WD=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}} * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]

; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended {{ill|Compulsory education|en|Compulsory education|WD=Q511009|lt=Compulsory education|1=Compulsory education}} term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ({{ill|Yoka|en|Yoka|WD=Q20661847|lt=Yoka|1=Yoka}}) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中学校令]]
'''Chūgakkōrei''' (中学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of February 7, Meiji 32) regulated [[secondary education institutions]] in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]], specifically governing [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] (commonly referred to as old-system middle schools).
== History == The first Chūgakkōrei was promulgated under Minister of Education {{ill|Mori Arinori|en|Mori Arinori|WD=Q1194472|lt=Mori Arinori|1=Mori Arinori}} on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) as part of the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]]. On [[February 7, 1899]], it was completely revised (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) and remained in effect until the [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 36 of Shōwa 18), promulgated on [[January 21, 1943]], replaced it.
== First Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15 of Meiji 19) ; Summary * Defined middle schools as institutions providing education for "those wishing to engage in practical work or enter higher schools." * Divided middle schools into '''[[Kōtō Chūgakkō]]''' (Higher Middle Schools) and '''[[Jinjo Chūgakkō]]''' (Ordinary Middle Schools): ** '''Higher Middle Schools:''' ** Managed by the Ministry of Education with five schools established nationwide. ** Expenses were funded through national and local taxes. ** '''Ordinary Middle Schools:''' ** Each prefecture could establish one school, with expenses covered by local funds. Multiple schools per prefecture were prohibited under the "one-prefecture-one-school" principle. ** The ordinance specified the curriculum, term length, and eligibility: ** Five-year program divided into five grades, each lasting one year. ** Admission for students aged 12 or older who graduated from elementary schools or equivalent institutions. ** Curriculum comprised 15 general subjects, with optional courses like agriculture or commerce. ; Partial Revisions * [[December 14, 1891]]: ** Relaxed the establishment conditions for Jinjo Chūgakkō: ** Allowed multiple schools per prefecture or none if deemed unnecessary. ** Allowed municipal governments to establish schools without hindering elementary education. ** Permitted vocational courses in agriculture, commerce, or industry.
== Second Chūgakkōrei == ; Promulgation Date * [[February 7, 1899]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 28 of Meiji 32) ; Key Changes * Renamed Jinjo Chūgakkō to simply '''Chūgakkō'''<ref>In {{ill|1893|en|1893|WD=Q7840|lt=1893|1=1893}} (Meiji 27), the [[Kōtōgakkōrei]] was issued, separating Kōtō Chūgakkō into [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]], leaving only Jinjo Chūgakkō under Chūgakkōrei.</ref>. * Defined the purpose of Chūgakkō as "providing higher general education for boys." * Retained a five-year term, with an optional one-year su11lementary course. * Admission criteria: ** Students aged 12 or older who completed the second grade of [[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]. * Mandated at least one Chūgakkō per prefecture, with provisions for additional schools at the Minister of Education's discretion. * Allowed municipal governments and school associations to establish schools more easily. * Authorized establishment of one branch school per Chūgakkō with ministerial a11roval. * Removed references to [[Kōtō Jogakkō]] (High Schools for Girls), as the [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] was issued simultaneously. ; Results * Increased o11ortunities for Chūgakkō expansion, stimulating interest in higher education and rapid growth in middle schools.
; Partial Revisions * [[July 18, 1907]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 280 of Meiji 40): ** Updated admission requirements to align with the extended {{ill|Compulsory education|en|Compulsory education|WD=Q511009|lt=Compulsory education|1=Compulsory education}} term<ref>Mandatory education was extended from four years (Jinjo Shōgakkō) to six years under the [[Shōgakkōrei]] revision.</ref>. ** Required graduates of six-year Jinjo Shōgakkō for Chūgakkō admission. * [[February 7, 1919]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 11 of Taishō 8): ** Expanded authority for school associations to establish Chūgakkō. ** Allowed establishment of preparatory courses ({{ill|Yoka|en|Yoka|WD=Q20661847|lt=Yoka|1=Yoka}}) within Chūgakkō for students meeting elementary school equivalency criteria. ** "Regulations for Enforcement of Chūgakkōrei" (March 29, 1919) set preparatory course term at two years for students aged 10 or older. * [[March 1, 1941]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 151 of Shōwa 16): ** Amended admission requirements to align with [[Kokumin Gakkō]] under the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]].
== References == {{References}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Chūgakkōrei}} * {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|WD=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}} * [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] * [[Kyūsei Kōtōgakkō]] * [[Kōtō Jogakkōrei]] * [[Jitsugyō Gakkōrei]] * [[Kōtō Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Chūtōgakkōrei]] * [[School Education Act]]
